On the **negative image**, these spots become **dark spots on a light background**, providing superior visual contrast to the radiologist. This inversion helps in:
- Better edge definition of microcalcification clusters
- Detection of calcifications as small as 100–500 μm
- Improved diagnostic accuracy in early breast cancer screening

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Histogram Equalization Pitfall:** Many students forget to **round** the computed $s_k$ values to the nearest integer. KTU examiners deduct **1 full mark** if rounding is omitted, since the output must be a valid gray level.
> 2. **Gamma Formula Pitfall:** The formula is $s = c \cdot r^{\gamma}$, **NOT** $s = c \cdot \gamma^r$. Confusing the position of $\gamma$ (in the exponent vs. the base) is a common error.
> 3. **Negative Formula Pitfall:** Students often write $s = L - r$ instead of $s = (L-1) - r$. The "$-1$" is critical for an 8-bit image with range $[0, 255]$.
> 4. **Boundary Condition Pitfall:** In contrast stretching, students frequently forget to specify that $r < r_1$ maps to $0$ and $r > r_2$ maps to $L-1$ — losing 2 marks.
> 5. **CDF Cap Pitfall:** CDF values can exceed 1.0 due to rounding — students must **explicitly cap CDF at 1.000** before multiplying by $(L-1)$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **Core Equation:** $s = T(r)$ — single-pixel dependency, no neighborhood involved.
- ✅ **Image Negative:** $s = (L-1) - r$ — used in mammography, fracture detection.
- ✅ **Log Transform:** $s = c \cdot \log(1+r)$ — compresses high intensities, expands low intensities.
- ✅ **Gamma Transform:** $s = c \cdot r^{\gamma}$ — $\gamma < 1$ brightens, $\gamma > 1$ darkens, $\gamma = 1$ is identity.
- ✅ **Contrast Stretch:** Piecewise linear map from $[r_1, r_2] \to [0, L-1]$.
- ✅ **Intensity Slicing:** Isolates a specific intensity band $[a, b]$.
- ✅ **Bit-plane Slicing:** 8 binary planes for an 8-bit image; MSB (bit 7) contains most visual info.
- ✅ **Histogram Equalization:** $s_k = (L-1) \cdot \sum_{j=0}^{k} \frac{n_j}{N}$ — produces approximately uniform output histogram.
- ✅ **Thresholding:** $s = 0$ if $r < T$, else $s = 255$ — binary segmentation.
- ✅ **CDF Properties:** Monotonically non-decreasing; maps $[0, 1] \to [0, 1]$; must be capped at 1.0 after rounding.
- ✅ **Medical Relevance:** Real-time, no edge distortion, DICOM compatible, FPGA implementable.
- ✅ **KTU High-Yield Topics for 2024:** Histogram equalization derivation, gamma correction plots, contrast stretching piecewise formulas.
- ✅ **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Never confuse power-law ($r^{\gamma}$) with exponential ($\gamma^r$).
- ✅ **Units & Constants:** $L = 256$, range $[0, 255]$, $\gamma = 2.2$ for sRGB display standard.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Contrast Stretching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBBMT604 - PRINCIPLES OF MEDICAL IMAGE PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Contrast Stretching # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Contrast Stretching** is a **point processing** (pixel-by-pixel) **spatial domain image enhancement technique** that operates on a single image to expand the dynamic range of its gray-level intensities. It applies a **piecewise linear transformation function** to remap a narrow input intensity range (e.g., a clinical MRI slice in which soft tissue occupies only gray values 60–140) into the full permissible output range of the display device, thereby amplifying local contrast and improving diagnostic visibility. In strict KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, contrast stretching belongs to the family of **gray-level transformations** applied to the histogram of the image. It is mathematically represented as: $$s = T(r)$$ where $r$ is the input pixel intensity, $s$ is the output (stretched) pixel intensity, and $T(\cdot)$ is a **monotonically increasing piecewise linear mapping function**. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 – Image Enhancement in Spatial Domain):** > Contrast stretching is treated under "Intensity (Gray-Level) Transformations" alongside image negative, log transformation, power-law (gamma) transformation, and histogram equalization. KTU examiners frequently use contrast stretching to test the student's ability to compute the **slope and intercept** of each linear segment. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are a **radiologist** reading a chest X-ray on a dimly lit viewbox. The actual film has subtle gray shades between the ribs that all look "the same gray" because they have been recorded in a narrow band of brightness — say between values 70 and 130 on a 0–255 scale. You *know* there is diagnostic information in those ribs (micro-fractures, infiltrates), but your eye cannot resolve it. **Contrast stretching is like adjusting the brightness knobs of that viewbox in a smart way**: every gray value below 70 is pushed down to pure black (0), every gray value above 130 is pushed up to pure white (255), and the gray values between 70 and 130 are mathematically "spread out" so that 70 becomes 0, 130 becomes 255, and the values in between fan out linearly. After this operation, the ribs become starkly visible. > [!TIP] > **Plain-English Takeaway:** Contrast stretching = "make a faded photocopy darker and brighter in the right places so the hidden details pop out." It does **not** create new information; it only re-distributes existing intensity values across a wider range. ## 1.3 Why Contrast Stretching is Critical in Medical Imaging - **L = 256** for standard 8-bit grayscale DICOM displays. - Most diagnostic features (lesions, tumors, fractures) occupy **< 25%** of the available dynamic range in raw acquisition. - Stretching is **reversible** (lossless information-theoretically within the original band). - It is **computationally cheap** — O(N) per pixel — making it suitable for real-time ultrasound and fluoroscopy pipelines. > [!NOTE] > **Key Physical Metric to Remember:** For an 8-bit medical image, the full displayable range is **[0, 255]**, i.e., **L = 256 discrete intensity levels**. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Concept – Decomposed into Logic Steps A contrast stretching operation is fully specified by **two input anchor points** $(r_1, s_1)$ and $(r_2, s_2)$ plus the global input/output endpoints $(r_{min}, s_{min})$ and $(r_{max}, s_{max})$. **Step 1 – Anchor Selection (Clinical Interpretation):** The radiologist or technician inspects the histogram and selects two pivot gray values $r_1$ and $r_2$ that bracket the diagnostically important tissue intensities. For example, in a brain MRI: - $r_1 = 40$ → below this = background (air/skull) - $r_2 = 180$ → above this = saturated CSF / fat **Step 2 – Output Anchor Assignment:** - $s_1$ = desired output for $r_1$ (often **0** → black) - $s_2$ = desired output for $r_2$ (often **L − 1 = 255** → white) **Step 3 – Piecewise Linear Mapping Function Construction:** $$s = T(r) = \begin{cases} \alpha_1 \, r + \beta_1, & 0 \le r < r_1 \\ \alpha_2 \, r + \beta_2, & r_1 \le r \le r_2 \\ \alpha_3 \, r + \beta_3, & r_2 < r \le r_{\max} \end{cases}$$ **Step 4 – Slope Calculation per Segment (the "How"):** The slope of each segment is the **ratio of output range to input range** for that segment. $$\alpha_k = \frac{s_{k,\text{end}} - s_{k,\text{start}}}{r_{k,\text{end}} - r_{k,\text{start}}}$$ **Step 5 – Intercept Calculation:** Using the point-slope formula with the anchor coordinates: $$\beta_k = s_{k,\text{start}} - \alpha_k \, r_{k,\text{start}}$$ **Step 6 – Pixel-wise Application:** Every pixel of the input image is passed through $T(\cdot)$ to produce the output. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Why":** By choosing $\alpha_2 > 1$ in the middle segment, the contrast (local differences) between tissues that fall in $[r_1, r_2]$ is amplified. The outer segments, typically with $\alpha_1, \alpha_3 < 1$, compress the uninteresting background and saturation regions. ## 2.2 The Four Standard Variants of Contrast Stretching ### Variant A – Min-Max (Full-Range) Stretching The simplest and most frequently asked form. The full observed dynamic range is mapped to the full display range. $$s = \frac{r - r_{\min}}{r_{\max} - r_{\min}} \times (L - 1)$$ ### Variant B – Percentile Stretching Uses the **p-th** and **(100−p)-th** percentile values $r_p$ and $r_{100-p}$ instead of absolute min/max to be robust to outliers (e.g., metal artifacts in CT). $$s = \frac{r - r_p}{r_{100-p} - r_p} \times (L - 1)$$ ### Variant C – Piecewise Linear (Most General Form) Anchored on $(r_1, s_1)$ and $(r_2, s_2)$ with user-defined slopes. ### Variant D – Clipped Linear Stretching Any $r < r_1 \Rightarrow s = 0$, any $r > r_2 \Rightarrow s = 255$. Only the band $[r_1, r_2]$ is linearly stretched. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Symbol** | **Meaning** | **Typical Value / Range** | **Unit** | |---|---|---|---| | $r$ | Input pixel intensity | $0 \le r \le 255$ | gray-level | | $s$ | Output pixel intensity | $0 \le s \le 255$ | gray-level | | $L$ | Total number of intensity levels | **256** (8-bit) | levels | | $r_{\min}, r_{\max}$ | Min and max of input image | computed from image | gray-level | | $r_1, r_2$ | Lower & upper anchor points | user-selected | gray-level | | $s_1, s_2$ | Corresponding output anchors | often 0 and 255 | gray-level | | $\alpha_k$ | Slope of segment $k$ | typically 0.5 to 4.0 | dimensionless | | $\beta_k$ | Intercept of segment $k$ | computed | gray-level | | $r_p$ | p-th percentile of input | e.g., $p = 2$ | gray-level | > [!WARNING] > **Markdown Safety Note:** Absolute values are written as `\vert x \vert` to avoid breaking the table syntax. Do **NOT** use the bare pipe character `|x|` inside a table cell — it will collapse the column structure in most markdown renderers. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | **Application Domain** | **Use of Contrast Stretching** | |---|---| | **Diagnostic Radiology** (X-ray, CT, MRI) | Enhances subtle soft-tissue differences for tumor detection | | **Mammography** | Standard preprocessing before CAD (Computer-Aided Detection) | | **Histopathology** | Stretched H&E stained slide images for cellular boundary detection | | **Endoscopy** | Real-time enhancement in minimally invasive surgery displays | | **Telemedicine** | Standardization across acquisition devices with varying dynamic ranges | | **Satellite / Remote Sensing** | (Non-medical analogue) – shoreline and vegetation demarcation | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 – Min-Max Contrast Stretching Transfer Function **Problem Setup:** Given an input image with intensities $r \in [r_{\min}, r_{\max}]$, derive the transformation that linearly maps this range onto $[0, L-1]$. **Step 1:** Define the desired mapping as a line $s = \alpha r + \beta$. $$s = \alpha r + \beta$$ **Step 2:** Apply the two endpoint conditions. $$s(r_{\min}) = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 0 = \alpha \, r_{\min} + \beta$$ $$s(r_{\max}) = L - 1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; L - 1 = \alpha \, r_{\max} + \beta$$ **Step 3:** Subtract the first equation from the second to eliminate $\beta$. $$\begin{aligned} (L - 1) - 0 &= \alpha \, r_{\max} + \beta - (\alpha \, r_{\min} + \beta) \\[4pt] L - 1 &= \alpha \, (r_{\max} - r_{\min}) \\[4pt] \alpha &= \frac{L - 1}{r_{\max} - r_{\min}} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4:** Substitute back to obtain $\beta$. $$\begin{aligned} \beta &= -\alpha \, r_{\min} \\[4pt] \beta &= -\left(\frac{L - 1}{r_{\max} - r_{\min}}\right) r_{\min} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5:** Write the closed-form transfer function. $$s = \left(\frac{L - 1}{r_{\max} - r_{\min}}\right) r - \left(\frac{L - 1}{r_{\max} - r_{\min}}\right) r_{\min}$$ **Step 6:** Factor out the common term $(L-1)$ for elegance. $$\boxed{\,s = \left(\frac{r - r_{\min}}{r_{\max} - r_{\min}}\right) (L - 1)\,}$$ This is the **canonical KTU answer** for the min-max contrast stretching formula. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 – Piecewise Linear Stretching for a 3-Segment Function **Problem Setup:** A CT image of the abdomen has intensity values in $[0, 200]$. The radiologist anchors the function at: - $(r_1 = 60, \, s_1 = 30)$ — lower threshold for soft tissue - $(r_2 = 140, \, s_2 = 220)$ — upper threshold for soft tissue - Outer endpoints: $(r = 0 \Rightarrow s = 0)$ and $(r = 200 \Rightarrow s = 255)$. Derive the three linear segments $s = \alpha_k r + \beta_k$. ### Segment 1 (Lower Tail): $r \in [0, 60] \rightarrow s \in [0, 30]$ **Slope:** $$\begin{aligned} \alpha_1 &= \frac{s_1 - 0}{r_1 - 0} \\[4pt] &= \frac{30 - 0}{60 - 0} \\[4pt] &= \frac{1}{2} = 0.5 \end{aligned}$$ **Intercept (using $s = \alpha_1 r + \beta_1$ at $r = 0, s = 0$):** $$\beta_1 = 0 - 0.5 \cdot 0 = 0$$ **Segment 1 equation:** $$\boxed{\,s = 0.5 \, r + 0 \quad \text{for} \quad 0 \le r < 60\,}$$ > **Logic Note:** Slope < 1 means contrast is *compressed* (background tissues appear flatter). This is intentional — they are not diagnostically interesting. ### Segment 2 (Middle / Diagnostic Band): $r \in [60, 140] \rightarrow s \in [30, 220]$ **Slope:** $$\begin{aligned} \alpha_2 &= \frac{s_2 - s_1}{r_2 - r_1} \\[4pt] &= \frac{220 - 30}{140 - 60} \\[4pt] &= \frac{190}{80} \\[4pt] &= \frac{19}{8} = 2.375 \end{aligned}$$ **Intercept (using anchor $(60, 30)$):** $$\begin{aligned} \beta_2 &= s_1 - \alpha_2 \, r_1 \\[4pt] &= 30 - (2.375)(60) \\[4pt] &= 30 - 142.5 \\[4pt] &= -112.5 \end{aligned}$$ **Segment 2 equation:** $$\boxed{\,s = 2.375 \, r - 112.5 \quad \text{for} \quad 60 \le r \le 140\,}$$ > **Logic Note:** Slope = 2.375 > 1 means contrast is *amplified* by a factor of ~2.4× in this critical diagnostic band. Small intensity differences in soft tissue are now mapped to large visual differences. ### Segment 3 (Upper Tail): $r \in [140, 200] \rightarrow s \in [220, 255]$ **Slope:** $$\begin{aligned} \alpha_3 &= \frac{255 - 220}{200 - 140} \\[4pt] &= \frac{35}{60} \\[4pt] &= \frac{7}{12} \approx 0.5833 \end{aligned}$$ **Intercept (using anchor $(140, 220)$):** $$\begin{aligned} \beta_3 &= 220 - (0.5833)(140) \\[4pt] &= 220 - 81.666 \\[4pt] &\approx 138.333 \end{aligned}$$ **Segment 3 equation:** $$\boxed{\,s = 0.5833 \, r + 138.333 \quad \text{for} \quad 140 < r \le 200\,}$$ ### Continuity Verification A correctly designed piecewise function must be **continuous** at the anchor points. Let us verify at $r = r_1 = 60$: $$\begin{aligned} \text{Limit from left: } & s(60^-) = 0.5(60) = 30 \\ \text{Limit from right: } & s(60^+) = 2.375(60) - 112.5 = 142.5 - 112.5 = 30 \end{aligned}$$ Both limits equal **30** ✓. Similarly at $r = r_2 = 140$: $$\begin{aligned} \text{Limit from left: } & s(140^-) = 2.375(140) - 112.5 = 332.5 - 112.5 = 220 \\ \text{Limit from right: } & s(140^+) = 0.5833(140) + 138.333 = 81.666 + 138.333 = 219.999 \approx 220 \end{aligned}$$ Continuity is preserved (within rounding error) ✓. ### Complete Mapping Function (Final Answer) $$s = T(r) = \begin{cases} 0.5 \, r, & 0 \le r < 60 \\[4pt] 2.375 \, r - 112.5, & 60 \le r \le 140 \\[4pt] 0.5833 \, r + 138.333, & 140 < r \le 200 \end{cases}$$ --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix – Worked Numerical Example **Question:** An X-ray image has input intensities in the range $[50, 180]$. Apply min-max stretching to map this to $[0, 255]$. Find the output intensity for an input pixel with $r = 110$. **Step 1:** Identify parameters. $$r_{\min} = 50, \quad r_{\max} = 180, \quad L - 1 = 255$$ **Step 2:** Apply the formula. $$\begin{aligned} s &= \left(\frac{r - r_{\min}}{r_{\max} - r_{\min}}\right)(L - 1) \\[4pt] &= \left(\frac{110 - 50}{180 - 50}\right)(255) \\[4pt] &= \left(\frac{60}{130}\right)(255) \\[4pt] &= (0.4615)(255) \\[4pt] &= 117.69 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3:** Round to nearest integer (since output must be a valid 8-bit value). $$\boxed{\,s \approx 118\,}$$ > **Interpretation:** A medium-gray pixel at $r = 110$ (which sat in the *middle* of the original narrow band) is mapped to $s \approx 118$ — close to mid-gray of the new stretched scale. This confirms that the stretching operation correctly preserves the relative ordering of pixels while expanding the dynamic range. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram – Contrast Stretching Pipeline The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates the end-to-end processing pipeline of a contrast stretching operation as it would appear in a clinical imaging workstation (e.g., PACS viewer with preprocessing hooks). ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Medical Image] --> B[Histogram Analysis Module] B --> C{Anchor Point Selection} C -->|Min-Max Auto| D1[Compute r_min and r_max] C -->|Percentile| D2[Compute r_p and r_100p] C -->|Manual Clinical| D3[Radiologist Sets r1 s1 r2 s2] D1 --> E[Slope Calculator] D2 --> E D3 --> E E --> F[Build Piecewise Linear Function T of r] F --> G[Pixel-wise Mapping s = T of r] G --> H[Contrast Stretched Output Image] H --> I[Display on Diagnostic Monitor] H --> J[Store in PACS Archive] style A fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b style H fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style F fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 ``` > **Reading Guide:** The yellow node `Build Piecewise Linear Function T(r)` is the **mathematical core** of the technique — every other block exists only to feed parameters into it or to apply its output. --- ## 4.2 Transformation Function Plot (Conceptual Schematic) The diagram below shows the **characteristic shape** of a piecewise linear contrast stretching function. The middle segment has slope > 1 (amplification), the outer segments have slope < 1 (compression). ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Input r = 0, Output s = 0] -->|Slope alpha_1 less than 1| P2[Anchor r1 s1] P2 -->|Slope alpha_2 greater than 1 DIAGNOSTIC BAND| P3[Anchor r2 s2] P3 -->|Slope alpha_3 less than 1| P4[Input r_max, Output s_max] style P2 fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17 style P3 fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17 style P1 fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#424242 style P4 fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#424242 ``` **Transfer Curve Sketch (for student notebooks):** ``` s 255 | * | * 220 | * ← (r2=140, s2=220) | * | * | * 30 |* * | * | * | * | * 0 *______________________________ 0 60 140 200 r ``` > **Reading Guide:** The middle segment is visibly **steeper** than the outer segments. This steepness IS the contrast amplification. The student should sketch exactly such a figure when answering KTU theory questions. --- ## 4.3 Histogram Transformation Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR H1[Input Histogram
Narrow band
e.g. 60 to 140] -->|Apply s = T r| H2[Output Histogram
Wide band
0 to 255] H1 --> X[Anchor r1 = 60] H1 --> Y[Anchor r2 = 140] X --> H2 Y --> H2 style H1 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c style H2 fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20 ``` > **Key Visual Insight:** The horizontal spread of the histogram is *physically widened*. The peak shape is **preserved** because stretching is a one-to-one (monotonic) mapping — no two input intensities are merged into one output intensity. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper] **Define contrast stretching in image processing. Mention any two situations in medical imaging where it is applied.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (2 marks):** Contrast stretching is a **point processing technique** in the spatial domain that expands the **narrow dynamic range** of gray levels in an input image to the **full displayable range** of the output device using a **piecewise linear transformation function**. It is mathematically expressed as: $$s = T(r)$$ where $T(\cdot)$ is a monotonically increasing piecewise linear function that maps input pixel intensities $r$ to output intensities $s$. **Two applications (1 mark):** 1. **Mammography:** Stretching the narrow gray-level range of microcalcifications in dense breast tissue to make them visible against glandular background. 2. **MRI of the brain:** Enhancing subtle gray matter / white matter contrast for early detection of multiple sclerosis plaques. --- ### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Distinguish between contrast stretching and histogram equalization.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | **Aspect** | **Contrast Stretching** | **Histogram Equalization** | |---|---|---| | Mapping type | **Piecewise linear** (user-defined) | **Non-linear** (cumulative distribution function based) | | Histogram shape after operation | Shape preserved, only widened | **Flattened** to approximate uniform distribution | | Parameter control | Radiologist chooses $(r_1, s_1)$ and $(r_2, s_2)$ | **Automatic**, no user parameters | | Computational complexity | O(N) | O(N + L log L) due to CDF computation | | KTU preference | Direct slope–intercept problems | Probability and CDF problems | > **Award 3 marks** for clear distinction across at least three criteria. Vague one-line answers fetch only 1 mark. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Module Internal Choice) ### Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2 Choice 1] **(a)** Derive the expression for the **min-max contrast stretching** transfer function and explain its significance in medical imaging. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 6-bit medical image (L = 64) has pixel intensities in the range $[8, 52]$. Apply min-max stretching and compute the output for input pixel values: $r = 8, \; 30, \; 52$. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for (a): **Step 1:** Define the linear mapping. $$s = \alpha r + \beta$$ **Step 2:** Apply boundary conditions $s(r_{\min}) = 0$ and $s(r_{\max}) = L - 1$. **Step 3:** Solve for slope. $$\alpha = \frac{L - 1}{r_{\max} - r_{\min}}$$ **Step 4:** Solve for intercept. $$\beta = -\alpha \, r_{\min}$$ **Step 5:** Final formula. $$s = \left(\frac{r - r_{\min}}{r_{\max} - r_{\min}}\right)(L - 1)$$ **Significance (2 marks):** - Expands the narrow acquisition dynamic range to the full display range, making subtle diagnostic features visible. - Used as the **first preprocessing step** in most CAD (Computer-Aided Detection) pipelines for mammography, lung CT, and retinal fundus images. - Lossless within the original intensity band (no new information created, only redistribution). #### Model Solution for (b): **Given:** $L = 64, \;\; r_{\min} = 8, \;\; r_{\max} = 52$. **Compute slope:** $$\begin{aligned} \alpha &= \frac{L - 1}{r_{\max} - r_{\min}} \\[4pt] &= \frac{64 - 1}{52 - 8} \\[4pt] &= \frac{63}{44} \\[4pt] &= \frac{63}{44} \approx 1.432 \end{aligned}$$ **Compute intercept:** $$\begin{aligned} \beta &= -\alpha \, r_{\min} \\[4pt] &= -\frac{63}{44} \times 8 \\[4pt] &= -\frac{504}{44} \\[4pt] &\approx -11.455 \end{aligned}$$ **Transfer function:** $$s = \frac{63}{44} (r - 8)$$ **Case 1: $r = 8$** $$\begin{aligned} s &= \frac{63}{44}(8 - 8) = 0 \end{aligned}$$ **Case 2: $r = 30$** $$\begin{aligned} s &= \frac{63}{44}(30 - 8) = \frac{63 \times 22}{44} = \frac{1386}{44} = 31.5 \approx 32 \end{aligned}$$ **Case 3: $r = 52$** $$\begin{aligned} s &= \frac{63}{44}(52 - 8) = \frac{63 \times 44}{44} = 63 \end{aligned}$$ **Result Table (1 mark for tabulation):** | **Input $r$** | **Output $s$ (exact)** | **Output $s$ (rounded)** | |---|---|---| | 8 | 0 | 0 | | 30 | 31.5 | 32 | | 52 | 63 | 63 | > [!NOTE] > **Verification:** Endpoints map perfectly: $r = 8 \rightarrow s = 0$ ✓, $r = 52 \rightarrow s = 63 = L-1$ ✓. The middle value $r = 30$ (midpoint of input range) maps to $s = 31.5 \approx 32$ (midpoint of output range) ✓. --- ### Question B [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2 Choice 2] **(a)** Explain **piecewise linear contrast stretching** with a neat sketch of the transformation function. Discuss how the choice of anchor points $(r_1, s_1)$ and $(r_2, s_2)$ affects the output image. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For a CT image, the piecewise transformation function is defined as: $$s = T(r) = \begin{cases} 0.2 \, r, & 0 \le r \le 50 \\ 1.5 \, r - 65, & 50 < r \le 150 \\ 0.5 \, r + 145, & 150 < r \le 255 \end{cases}$$ Determine: **(i)** the anchor points $(r_1, s_1)$ and $(r_2, s_2)$; **(ii)** whether the function is continuous; **(iii)** the slope of each segment and its physical meaning. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for (a): **Definition (2 marks):** Piecewise linear contrast stretching is a generalization of min-max stretching in which the transformation function $T(r)$ is composed of **multiple linear segments** joined at user-defined anchor points. The general form is: $$s = T(r) = \begin{cases} \alpha_1 r + \beta_1, & r \in [r_0, r_1] \\ \alpha_2 r + \beta_2, & r \in [r_1, r_2] \\ \alpha_3 r + \beta_3, & r \in [r_2, r_3] \end{cases}$$ **Sketch (2 marks):** *[Student should draw a 3-segment piecewise linear curve with the middle segment visibly steeper than the outer two — see Section 4.2 of these notes for the reference figure.]* **Effect of Anchor Points (3 marks):** - **Lower anchor $(r_1, s_1)$:** All pixels with $r < r_1$ are mapped to $[0, s_1]$. Choosing $s_1 = 0$ makes everything below $r_1$ pure black (clipping background). - **Upper anchor $(r_2, s_2)$:** All pixels with $r > r_2$ are mapped to $[s_2, L-1]$. Choosing $s_2 = 255$ makes everything above $r_2$ pure white (clipping saturation). - **Middle slope $\alpha_2$:** Controls the **amplification factor** for the diagnostic band. Larger $\alpha_2$ = stronger contrast enhancement but risk of **noise amplification**. - **Wrong anchor selection** can either suppress diagnostically critical tissue or push noise into clinically misleading features. #### Model Solution for (b): **(i) Anchor Points (2 marks):** At $r = r_1 = 50$, two segments meet. Compute $s$ from either segment: - From Segment 1: $s = 0.2 \times 50 = 10$ - From Segment 2: $s = 1.5 \times 50 - 65 = 75 - 65 = 10$ ✓ So $(r_1, s_1) = (50, 10)$. At $r = r_2 = 150$, two segments meet. Compute $s$: - From Segment 2: $s = 1.5 \times 150 - 65 = 225 - 65 = 160$ - From Segment 3: $s = 0.5 \times 150 + 145 = 75 + 145 = 220$ These are **different** (160 ≠ 220), so there is a **jump discontinuity** at $r = 150$. **Discontinuity magnitude:** $$\Delta s = 220 - 160 = 60 \text{ gray levels}$$ **(ii) Continuity Check (2 marks):** - At $r = 50$: $s$ is continuous (both segments give $s = 10$). ✓ - At $r = 150$: $s$ is **discontinuous** with a jump of magnitude **60**. ✗ **Comment:** A discontinuity of 60 levels will appear as a **visible band/edge artifact** in the stretched image, mimicking a false anatomical boundary. This is clinically undesirable. **(iii) Slopes and Physical Meaning (3 marks):** | **Segment** | **Range of $r$** | **Slope $\alpha_k$** | **Physical Meaning** | |---|---|---|---| | 1 (Lower tail) | $[0, 50]$ | **0.2** | Strong compression (×0.2) of background/noise region | | 2 (Diagnostic band) | $(50, 150]$ | **1.5** | Moderate contrast amplification (×1.5) for soft tissue | | 3 (Upper tail) | $(150, 255]$ | **0.5** | Mild compression (×0.5) of saturated/calcified regions | > **Interpretation:** The diagnostic band is amplified 1.5×, meaning a 1-unit intensity difference in the original image becomes a 1.5-unit difference in the displayed image — increasing visual contrast without saturating the display. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Contrast Stretching Questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting to use $(L-1)$** instead of $L$ in the denominator's companion. Many students write $\frac{r - r_{\min}}{r_{\max} - r_{\min}} \times L$ instead of $\times (L-1)$. This costs **2 marks** instantly in a 14-mark problem. > > 2. **Mixing up "stretching" with "histogram equalization".** Stretching is *piecewise linear and parameter-controlled*. Equalization is *non-linear and CDF-based*. KTU examiners deduct up to **3 marks** for confused definitions. > > 3. **Failing to verify continuity at anchor points.** Always plug $r_1$ and $r_2$ into *both* adjacent segments and show they give identical $s$ values. Continuity is worth **2 marks** as a separate assessment criterion. > > 4. **Not rounding the output to the nearest integer** when the question involves an 8-bit image. Final $s$ must be an integer in $[0, 255]$. Writing $s = 117.69$ without rounding is penalized. > > 5. **Confusing $r_{\min}$ with $r_1$.** $r_{\min}$ is the absolute minimum of the *image*. $r_1$ is a *user-selected* anchor. They are equal **only** in min-max stretching. In piecewise stretching they are different. > > 6. **Forgetting to draw the transformation curve** in theory questions. KTU valuation keys explicitly allocate **1–2 marks** for the sketch. A perfect algebraic derivation without a sketch is docked. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ✅ Contrast stretching is a **point processing / spatial domain / piecewise linear** technique. - ✅ The canonical min-max formula is $s = \frac{r - r_{\min}}{r_{\max} - r_{\min}} \times (L - 1)$. - ✅ For an 8-bit image, **L = 256** and the output range is **[0, 255]**. - ✅ Slope of a linear segment: $\alpha = \frac{\Delta s}{\Delta r} = \frac{s_2 - s_1}{r_2 - r_1}$. - ✅ Intercept: $\beta = s_1 - \alpha \, r_1$ (or $\beta = s_2 - \alpha \, r_2$ — both must give the same value). - ✅ **Slope > 1** in the diagnostic band = **contrast amplification**. - ✅ **Slope < 1** in outer tails = **contrast compression** (suppression of background/saturation). - ✅ Always **verify continuity** at anchor points: limits from left and right must match. - ✅ Contrast stretching is **monotonic** and **one-to-one** — no two input pixels collide at the same output. - ✅ It is **reversible** (no information loss within the original intensity band) and **O(N)** in computation. - ✅ Clinical applications: **mammography, brain MRI, chest X-ray, abdominal CT, retinal fundus, histopathology**. - ✅ Four variants to remember: **Min-Max, Percentile, Piecewise Linear, Clipped Linear**. - ✅ Unlike histogram equalization, contrast stretching does **not flatten** the histogram — it only **widens** it. - ✅ Always present the final output as an **integer** for 8-bit / 6-bit / 4-bit medical images. - ✅ Draw the **transfer function sketch** in every theory answer — KTU examiners explicitly reward it.

topics.title: Clipping and Thresholding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBMT604 - PRINCIPLES OF MEDICAL IMAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Clipping and Thresholding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Clipping — Formal Academic Definition

In the context of medical image processing, **intensity clipping** is a point-processing operation that restricts (limits) the intensity values of all pixels in an image to a defined dynamic range bounded by a lower limit $L_{low}$ and an upper limit $L_{high}$. Any pixel value falling outside this interval is *saturated* to the nearest boundary, while values within the interval are preserved.

Mathematically, the clipping transformation $T: f(x,y) \rightarrow g(x,y)$ is defined as:

$$
g(x,y) = \begin{cases}
L_{low} & \text{if } f(x,y) < L_{low} \\
f(x,y) & \text{if } L_{low} \leq f(x,y) \leq L_{high} \\
L_{high} & \text{if } f(x,y) > L_{high}
\end{cases}
$$

The selection of the clipping range $(L_{low}, L_{high})$ is typically guided by a **histogram analysis** of the medical image, since anatomical structures of interest (e.g., bone, soft tissue, air in CT) occupy characteristic intensity intervals in Hounsfield Units (HU).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology — Dynamic Range**: The difference between the maximum and minimum representable intensity values. For a standard 8-bit medical image, the dynamic range is **256 gray levels** (0–255).

### 1.2 Thresholding — Formal Academic Definition

**Thresholding** is the simplest and most widely used method of **image segmentation**. It converts a grayscale image $f(x,y)$ into a binary image $g(x,y)$ by comparing each pixel intensity with one or more threshold values $T$. Pixels satisfying the threshold criterion are assigned to the foreground (object/region of interest), while others become background.

For the simplest case of **single global thresholding**:

$$
g(x,y) = \begin{cases}
1 & \text{if } f(x,y) \geq T \\
0 & \text{if } f(x,y) < T
\end{cases}
$$

In multi-level thresholding with $n$ thresholds $T_1 < T_2 < \cdots < T_n$, the output is an $n$-level labeled image.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight**: Thresholding is the **foundation of medical image segmentation** — it is the first step in detecting tumors, organs, bone contours, and lesions from radiographic, CT, MRI, and ultrasound images.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Clipping — The Window-View Analogy:**

> Imagine you are looking at a vast landscape through a small window in your room. The landscape has an infinite range of colors and brightnesses. By *clipping*, you decide to look only at the *blues of the sky* — any pixel darker than navy blue is forced to black (clipped at the lower end), and any pixel whiter than cloud-white is forced to pure white (clipped at the upper end). Everything in between is faithfully reproduced. In medical imaging, this is analogous to choosing the **window width** and **window level** on a CT console to highlight specific tissue types.

**Thresholding — The Traffic-Light Analogy:**

> Imagine scanning a black-and-white photograph with a torch (flashlight) that has a brightness dial set to a specific level $T$. Anything brighter than the dial setting reflects light back to you (becomes **white = 1**), and anything darker absorbs the light (becomes **black = 0**). This is exactly what thresholding does — it converts a continuous-tone grayscale image into a binary *mask* that separates structures of interest from the background. In medical imaging, thresholding is how we separate a tumor (bright) from healthy tissue (dark) on a CT scan.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard Display Range (8-bit)**: $L_{min} = 0$, $L_{max} = 255$
- **CT Hounsfield Unit Range**: Air $= -1000$ HU, Water $= 0$ HU, Bone $\approx +1000$ HU
- **MRI Intensity Range**: Arbitrary units, normalized to 12-bit (0–4095) in DICOM

> [!NOTE]
> The **Window Level (WL)** and **Window Width (WW)** in CT imaging are direct engineering implementations of *intensity clipping* + *linear rescaling*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Types of Intensity Clipping

Clipping operations in medical image processing fall into three principal categories:

**A. Hard Clipping (Saturation Clipping)** — Limits all pixel values to a strict range.

$$
g(x,y) = \text{clip}[f(x,y), L_{low}, L_{high}]
$$

**B. Percentage Clipping (Histogram-Based Clipping)** — A dynamic technique where $L_{low}$ and $L_{high}$ are chosen so that a specified percentile (e.g., the lowest 1% and highest 1%) of histogram pixels are clipped. This is robust to **outliers** such as metal artifacts.

**C. Window/Level Clipping (Linear Windowing)** — Maps a sub-range $[WL - WW/2, \ WL + WW/2]$ of the original intensity to the full display range $[0, 255]$ using linear scaling *and* clipping simultaneously.

$$
g(x,y) = 255 \cdot \frac{f(x,y) - (WL - WW/2)}{WW}
$$

with values outside the window clamped to 0 or 255.

### 2.2 Classification of Thresholding Techniques

A systematic taxonomy used throughout KTU-evaluated answers:

**1. Global Thresholding** — A single $T$ applied to the entire image. Works when illumination is uniform and object-background contrast is strong.

**2. Local / Adaptive Thresholding** — $T(x,y)$ varies spatially as a function of local neighborhood statistics (e.g., local mean, local Gaussian-weighted mean).

$$
T(x,y) = m(x,y) + C
$$

where $m(x,y)$ is the local mean and $C$ is a constant offset.

**3. Automatic Threshold Selection (Otsu, Kapur, Kittler)** — Algorithms that compute the optimal $T$ by maximizing a statistical criterion such as inter-class variance.

**4. Multi-level Thresholding** — Producing $n$ bands, used for multi-tissue segmentation (e.g., separating gray matter, white matter, and CSF in MRI brain images).

**5. Hysteresis Thresholding** — Two thresholds $T_{low}$ and $T_{high}$ are used; pixels above $T_{high}$ are definite edges, those between $T_{low}$ and $T_{high}$ are edges only if connected to definite-edge pixels.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Description | Boundary / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $g = \text{clip}[f, L_{low}, L_{high}]$ | Hard clipping (saturation) | $L_{low}, L_{high} \in [0, 255]$ for 8-bit |
| $g = 255 \cdot (f - L_{low}) / (L_{high} - L_{low})$ | Linear windowing (Window/Level) | $f \in [L_{low}, L_{high}]$ |
| $g = 1$ if $f \geq T$, else $0$ | Global single threshold | $T \in [0, 255]$ |
| $T(x,y) = m_{local}(x,y) + C$ | Adaptive (mean-based) thresholding | $C$ is a bias constant |
| $\sigma^2_B(t) = \omega_0 \omega_1 (\mu_0 - \mu_1)^2$ | Otsu's inter-class variance | $t$ is candidate threshold |
| $\omega_k(t) = \sum_{i=0}^{t} p_i$ | Class probability (Otsu) | $p_i$ = histogram probability |
| $\mu_k(t) = \sum_{i=0}^{t} i \cdot p_i / \omega_k$ | Class mean intensity (Otsu) | Dimensionless |
| $T^* = \arg\max_t \sigma^2_B(t)$ | Optimal threshold by Otsu | $T^* \in [0, L-1]$ |
| $H(t) = -\sum \omega_k \ln(\omega_k)$ | Kapur's maximum entropy criterion | $H$ in nats or bits |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical for KTU Board Exams**: When writing formulas inside tables, never use vertical pipe `|` for absolute value or conditional expressions. Always use $\vert$ or $\mid$ instead to prevent breaking the table syntax.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Radiology Workstations**: Radiologists interactively clip using WW/WL controls to evaluate lung nodules, bone fractures, and soft-tissue masses.
- **Computer-Aided Detection (CAD)**: Thresholding is the pre-processing step for mammographic microcalcification detection.
- **3D Printing of Organs**: Multi-level thresholding of CT/MRI scans converts grayscale voxels into STL files for anatomical models.
- **Surgical Navigation**: Adaptive thresholding isolates surgical tools (highly reflective) from tissue in endoscopic video.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Linear Windowing (Window/Level) Transformation

The **Window Level (WL)** is the center of the intensity range of interest, and the **Window Width (WW)** is the breadth of intensities displayed.

**Step 1**: Express the lower and upper intensity bounds in terms of WL and WW.

$$
L_{low} = WL - \frac{WW}{2}, \quad L_{high} = WL + \frac{WW}{2}
$$

**Step 2**: Apply a linear mapping to scale this window into the displayable range $[0, 255]$.

$$
g(x,y) = \left( \frac{f(x,y) - L_{low}}{L_{high} - L_{low}} \right) \times 255
$$

**Step 3**: Substitute $L_{low}$ and $L_{high}$:

$$
g(x,y) = \left( \frac{f(x,y) - \left(WL - \frac{WW}{2}\right)}{WW} \right) \times 255
$$

**Step 4**: Apply the clipping condition explicitly to enforce bounds.

$$
g(x,y) = \begin{cases}
0 & \text{if } f(x,y) < WL - \dfrac{WW}{2} \\
255 \cdot \dfrac{f(x,y) - WL + \dfrac{WW}{2}}{WW} & \text{if } WL - \dfrac{WW}{2} \leq f(x,y) \leq WL + \dfrac{WW}{2} \\
255 & \text{if } f(x,y) > WL + \dfrac{WW}{2}
\end{cases}
$$

> This is the exact mathematical formulation implemented in every clinical DICOM viewer (OsiriX, 3D Slicer, RadiAnt).

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of Otsu's Optimal Threshold

Otsu's method (1979) chooses the threshold $T^*$ that **maximizes the between-class variance** $\sigma^2_B$ of the two pixel classes (background and foreground).

**Step 1: Histogram normalization.**

Let $L$ be the total number of gray levels (typically 256). The normalized histogram is:

$$
p_i = \frac{n_i}{N}, \quad i = 0, 1, \dots, L-1
$$

where $n_i$ is the number of pixels with intensity $i$, and $N$ is the total pixel count.

**Step 2: Class probabilities at candidate threshold $t$.**

Class $C_0$ = background (pixels with intensity in $[0, t]$), and class $C_1$ = foreground (pixels with intensity in $[t+1, L-1]$):

$$
\omega_0(t) = \sum_{i=0}^{t} p_i, \quad \omega_1(t) = \sum_{i=t+1}^{L-1} p_i = 1 - \omega_0(t)
$$

**Step 3: Class mean intensities.**

$$
\mu_0(t) = \sum_{i=0}^{t} \frac{i \cdot p_i}{\omega_0(t)}, \quad \mu_1(t) = \sum_{i=t+1}^{L-1} \frac{i \cdot p_i}{\omega_1(t)}
$$

**Step 4: Global mean intensity.**

$$
\mu_T = \sum_{i=0}^{L-1} i \cdot p_i = \omega_0 \mu_0 + \omega_1 \mu_1
$$

**Step 5: Between-class variance derivation.**

The between-class variance is defined as:

$$
\sigma^2_B(t) = \omega_0(t) \cdot \omega_1(t) \cdot \left[ \mu_0(t) - \mu_1(t) \right]^2
$$

**Step 6: Equivalent expanded form.**

Using the identity $\mu_0 - \mu_1 = \dfrac{\mu_T - \mu_T}{\omega_0 \omega_1} \cdot \omega_0 \omega_1$, one can equivalently express:

$$
\sigma^2_B(t) = \frac{\left( \mu_T \cdot \omega_0(t) - \mu(t) \right)^2}{\omega_0(t) \left( 1 - \omega_0(t) \right)}
$$

where $\mu(t) = \sum_{i=0}^{t} i \cdot p_i$ is the zeroth-order cumulative moment.

**Step 7: Optimal threshold.**

The Otsu threshold is the value $T^*$ that maximizes the between-class variance:

$$
T^* = \arg\max_{t \in [0, L-1]} \sigma^2_B(t)
$$

**Step 8: Algorithmic implementation summary.**

The procedure is:
- (i) Compute histogram $p_i$ for $i = 0, \dots, 255$.
- (ii) Initialize $\omega_0(0) = p_0$ and $\mu(0) = 0$.
- (iii) For each candidate $t$, update cumulative sums and compute $\sigma^2_B(t)$.
- (iv) Track the maximum value of $\sigma^2_B(t)$ and corresponding $t$.
- (v) Output $t^* = T^*$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Between-Class Variance?** Maximizing $\sigma^2_B$ equivalently **minimizes the intra-class variance** $\sigma^2_W$. Since total variance $\sigma^2_T = \sigma^2_W + \sigma^2_B$ is constant, maximizing $\sigma^2_B$ ensures the two pixel classes are as statistically separable as possible.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Otsu's Method

Consider a 4-level image ($L = 4$) with histogram probabilities $p_0 = 0.4$, $p_1 = 0.3$, $p_2 = 0.2$, $p_3 = 0.1$.

**Step 1**: Global mean.

$$
\mu_T = (0)(0.4) + (1)(0.3) + (2)(0.2) + (3)(0.1) = 1.0
$$

**Step 2**: Evaluate candidate thresholds $t = 1, 2, 3$:

For $t = 1$:
$$
\omega_0 = 0.7, \quad \omega_1 = 0.3
$$
$$
\mu_0 = (0)(0.4/0.7) + (1)(0.3/0.7) = 0.4286
$$
$$
\mu_1 = (2)(0.2/0.3) + (3)(0.1/0.3) = 2.3333
$$
$$
\sigma^2_B(1) = (0.7)(0.3)(0.4286 - 2.3333)^2 = (0.21)(3.628) = 0.7619
$$

For $t = 2$:
$$
\omega_0 = 0.9, \quad \omega_1 = 0.1
$$
$$
\mu_0 = [(0)(0.4) + (1)(0.3) + (2)(0.2)] / 0.9 = 0.7/0.9 = 0.7778
$$
$$
\mu_1 = (3)(0.1)/0.1 = 3.0
$$
$$
\sigma^2_B(2) = (0.9)(0.1)(0.7778 - 3.0)^2 = (0.09)(4.938) = 0.4444
$$

For $t = 3$:
$$
\omega_0 = 1.0, \quad \omega_1 = 0 \rightarrow \text{undefined}
$$

**Step 3**: Maximum is at $t = 1$. Thus $T^* = 1$.

> This illustrates how Otsu's method selects the threshold that maximally separates the histogram into two distinct modes.

---

### 3.4 Adaptive Thresholding — Mathematical Formulation

In non-uniform illumination (typical in microscopy, fundus photography, and X-ray images), a single global threshold fails. **Adaptive thresholding** computes a local threshold per pixel using a neighborhood window of size $k \times k$.

$$
T(x,y) = \sum_{(m,n) \in W(x,y)} w(m,n) \cdot f(x+m, y+n) + C
$$

**Step 1**: For the **local mean** variant, $w(m,n) = \dfrac{1}{k^2}$.

$$
T_{mean}(x,y) = \frac{1}{k^2} \sum_{m} \sum_{n} f(x+m, y+n) + C
$$

**Step 2**: For the **Gaussian-weighted** variant, $w(m,n) = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sigma^2} \exp\left(-\dfrac{m^2 + n^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$.

$$
T_{gauss}(x,y) = \sum_{(m,n) \in W} \frac{1}{2\pi\sigma^2} \exp\left(-\frac{m^2 + n^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) f(x+m, y+n) + C
$$

**Step 3**: Binary output.

$$
g(x,y) = \begin{cases}
1 & \text{if } f(x,y) \geq T(x,y) \\
0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

The constant $C$ is a tuning parameter that compensates for differences in reflectivity/absorption between regions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Intensity Clipping Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Input Medical Image f of x y] --> B1{Is f of x y less than L low}
    B1 -- YES --> C1[Set g = L low]
    B1 -- NO --> D1{Is f of x y greater than L high}
    D1 -- YES --> C2[Set g = L high]
    D1 -- NO --> C3[Set g = f of x y unchanged]
    C1 --> E1[Output Clipped Image g of x y]
    C2 --> E1
    C3 --> E1
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Global Thresholding Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A2[Input Grayscale Image f of x y] --> B2[Select Threshold T]
    B2 --> C2{Compare f of x y with T}
    C2 -- f greater or equal T --> D2[Assign g = 1 Foreground]
    C2 -- f less than T --> E2[Assign g = 0 Background]
    D2 --> F2[Output Binary Mask]
    E2 --> F2
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Otsu Method Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A3[Input Image] --> B3[Compute Normalized Histogram p i]
    B3 --> C3[Initialize omega 0 and mu t at t equals 0]
    C3 --> D3[Iterate t from 1 to L minus 1]
    D3 --> E3[Update Cumulative omega and mu]
    E3 --> F3[Compute sigma squared B of t]
    F3 --> G3{sigma B of t greater than sigma B max}
    G3 -- YES --> H3[Update T star equals t]
    G3 -- NO --> D3
    H3 --> I3[Output Optimal Threshold T star]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Adaptive Thresholding Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A4[Input Grayscale Image f of x y] --> B4[Sliding Window of size k cross k]
    B4 --> C4[Compute Local Statistic]
    C4 --> D4{Choose Strategy}
    D4 -- STRATEGY 1 --> E4[Local Mean m of x y]
    D4 -- STRATEGY 2 --> F4[Gaussian Weighted Mean]
    D4 -- STRATEGY 3 --> G4[Local Median]
    E4 --> H4[Add Bias C]
    F4 --> H4
    G4 --> H4
    H4 --> I4[Generate Local Threshold T of x y]
    I4 --> J4{Compare f with T pixel wise}
    J4 -- f greater or equal T --> K4[Assign Binary 1]
    J4 -- f less than T --> L4[Assign Binary 0]
    K4 --> M4[Output Adaptive Binary Image]
    L4 --> M4
```

### 4.5 Comparative Classification Tree — Clipping vs Thresholding

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A5[Image Processing Operations on Intensity] --> B5[Clipping Type]
    A5 --> C5[Thresholding Type]
    B5 --> B6[Hard Clipping Saturation]
    B5 --> B7[Percentage Clipping Histogram Based]
    B5 --> B8[Window Level Clipping Linear Mapping]
    C5 --> C6[Global Thresholding]
    C5 --> C7[Adaptive Thresholding]
    C5 --> C8[Automatic Threshold Otsu Method]
    C5 --> C9[Multi level Thresholding]
    C5 --> C10[Hysteresis Thresholding]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: Define intensity clipping. List its three principal types. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
Intensity clipping is a point operation that constrains pixel intensity values to a specified range $[L_{low}, L_{high}]$. Values below $L_{low}$ are mapped to $L_{low}$; values above $L_{high}$ are mapped to $L_{high}$; values within are preserved.

The three principal types are:
1. **Hard Clipping (Saturation Clipping)** — strict enforcement of boundaries.
2. **Percentage Clipping** — boundaries chosen based on histogram percentiles.
3. **Window/Level Clipping** — linear mapping of a chosen sub-range to display range.

---

#### Question 2: What is Otsu's method? State the criterion it optimizes. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
Otsu's method is an **automatic global thresholding technique** proposed by Nobuyuki Otsu in 1979. It computes the optimal threshold $T^*$ by maximizing the **between-class variance** $\sigma^2_B(t)$ of foreground and background pixel classes, which equivalently minimizes their intra-class variance.

The optimization criterion is:

$$
T^* = \arg\max_{t \in [0, L-1]} \sigma^2_B(t) = \arg\max_t \left[ \omega_0(t) \cdot \omega_1(t) \cdot \left( \mu_0(t) - \mu_1(t) \right)^2 \right]
$$

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**: Students frequently lose marks by (i) forgetting to apply the *clipping condition* in Window/Level mapping — they write only the linear formula without the piecewise boundary conditions, (ii) confusing class probabilities $\omega_0$ with histogram values $p_i$, and (iii) omitting the maximization step in Otsu's derivation. Always present the full piecewise formulation and conclude with the $\arg\max$ operation.

---

#### Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the various intensity clipping techniques used in medical image processing with mathematical formulations and a suitable diagram. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

Intensity clipping is a point-processing operation that restricts pixel values to a specified intensity interval. The general clipping transformation is:

$$
g(x,y) = \begin{cases}
L_{low} & \text{if } f(x,y) < L_{low} \\
f(x,y) & \text{if } L_{low} \leq f(x,y) \leq L_{high} \\
L_{high} & \text{if } f(x,y) > L_{high}
\end{cases}
$$

**Type 1 — Hard Clipping (Saturation)**: Every pixel outside $[L_{low}, L_{high}]$ is saturated to the boundary. This is the simplest clipping and is used when a strict dynamic range is required, e.g., ensuring all values fit within 8 bits.

**Type 2 — Percentage Clipping**: The thresholds $L_{low}$ and $L_{high}$ are chosen such that a fixed percentage (typically 1–2%) of the lowest and highest histogram pixels are clipped. This eliminates outliers such as metal artifacts or noise spikes. The percentage-based formula:

$$
L_{low} = P_{\alpha/2}(\text{histogram}), \quad L_{high} = P_{100-\alpha/2}(\text{histogram})
$$

where $P$ denotes the percentile.

**Type 3 — Window/Level Clipping (CT Windowing)**: A linear mapping combined with clipping. Given window level $WL$ and window width $WW$:

$$
g(x,y) = \begin{cases}
0 & \text{if } f(x,y) < WL - \dfrac{WW}{2} \\
255 \cdot \dfrac{f(x,y) - WL + \dfrac{WW}{2}}{WW} & \text{if } WL - \dfrac{WW}{2} \leq f(x,y) \leq WL + \dfrac{WW}{2} \\
255 & \text{if } f(x,y) > WL + \dfrac{WW}{2}
\end{cases}
$$

**Application in CT**: For lung imaging, $WL = -600$ HU, $WW = 1500$ HU. For bone imaging, $WL = 400$ HU, $WW = 1800$ HU. For soft tissue, $WL = 50$ HU, $WW = 400$ HU.

The flow diagram in Section 4.1 illustrates the decision logic. Clipping is essential in **DICOM viewers** and is the foundation of all diagnostic radiological display.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** With detailed derivation, explain Otsu's automatic thresholding method. How does it maximize inter-class variance? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

Otsu's method finds the threshold $T^*$ that minimizes intra-class variance (or equivalently maximizes inter-class variance) of the two pixel classes.

**Derivation:**

Let $L$ be the number of gray levels, $p_i$ the normalized histogram probability at intensity $i$, $N$ the total pixel count.

**Step 1**: Class probabilities at candidate threshold $t$:

$$
\omega_0(t) = \sum_{i=0}^{t} p_i, \quad \omega_1(t) = \sum_{i=t+1}^{L-1} p_i
$$

**Step 2**: Class mean intensities:

$$
\mu_0(t) = \frac{\sum_{i=0}^{t} i \cdot p_i}{\omega_0(t)}, \quad \mu_1(t) = \frac{\sum_{i=t+1}^{L-1} i \cdot p_i}{\omega_1(t)}
$$

**Step 3**: Global mean:

$$
\mu_T = \sum_{i=0}^{L-1} i \cdot p_i = \omega_0 \mu_0 + \omega_1 \mu_1
$$

**Step 4**: Between-class variance:

$$
\sigma^2_B(t) = \omega_0(t) \omega_1(t) \left[ \mu_0(t) - \mu_1(t) \right]^2
$$

**Step 5**: Since total variance $\sigma^2_T = \sigma^2_W + \sigma^2_B$ is constant (independent of $t$), maximizing $\sigma^2_B$ is equivalent to minimizing intra-class variance $\sigma^2_W$. This means the threshold separates the two pixel classes with maximum statistical separability.

**Step 6**: The optimal threshold:

$$
T^* = \arg\max_{t \in [0, L-1]} \sigma^2_B(t)
$$

**Step 7**: Practical algorithm:
- Compute histogram $p_i$ of the image.
- For each candidate $t$ from $0$ to $L-1$:
   - Update cumulative sums $\omega_0(t)$ and $\mu(t) = \sum_{i=0}^{t} i \cdot p_i$.
   - Compute $\sigma^2_B(t) = \dfrac{(\mu_T \omega_0 - \mu)^2}{\omega_0 (1 - \omega_0)}$.
- Track maximum.
- Output $T^*$.

Otsu's method is **unsupervised**, requires no training data, and is widely used for segmenting medical images with bimodal histograms (e.g., liver lesions on CT, white/gray matter on MRI).

---

#### Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss global versus adaptive thresholding with their merits and demerits. Derive the local mean adaptive threshold formulation. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Global Thresholding** uses a single threshold $T$ for the entire image. Its formulation is:

$$
g(x,y) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } f(x,y) \geq T \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Merits**: Simple, fast (O(N) where N is pixel count), effective when illumination is uniform and histogram is bimodal.

**Demerits**: Fails when illumination varies across the image (e.g., X-ray images with non-uniform beam intensity, microscopy with vignetting).

**Adaptive Thresholding** computes a local threshold per pixel using neighborhood statistics. Its formulation:

$$
T(x,y) = \frac{1}{k^2} \sum_{(m,n) \in W(x,y)} f(x+m, y+n) + C
$$

where $W$ is a $k \times k$ window centered at $(x,y)$ and $C$ is a constant bias.

**Derivation**:

**Step 1**: Define the local mean within a sliding window:

$$
m(x,y) = \frac{1}{k^2} \sum_{m = -k/2}^{k/2} \sum_{n = -k/2}^{k/2} f(x+m, y+n)
$$

**Step 2**: Add bias constant $C$ to compensate for the typical intensity difference between object and background:

$$
T(x,y) = m(x,y) + C
$$

**Step 3**: Apply the binary thresholding rule:

$$
g(x,y) = \begin{cases}
1 & \text{if } f(x,y) \geq T(x,y) \\
0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

**Merits of Adaptive**: Handles non-uniform illumination, robust in microscopy, fundus imaging, and X-ray.

**Demerits of Adaptive**: Computationally expensive (O(Nk²)), sensitive to window size $k$, requires tuning of $C$.

**Variants**:
- **Gaussian-weighted**: Uses Gaussian kernel weights $w(m,n) = \frac{1}{2\pi\sigma^2} \exp\left(-\frac{m^2 + n^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ instead of uniform weights.
- **Local median**: Uses median instead of mean — more robust to salt-and-pepper noise.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain multi-level thresholding and its application in medical imaging. How does the Kapur entropy criterion differ from Otsu's method? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**Multi-level Thresholding** extends binary thresholding by using $n$ thresholds to produce $n+1$ intensity bands, enabling segmentation of multiple tissue types.

For $n$ thresholds $T_1 < T_2 < \dots < T_n$:

$$
g(x,y) = \begin{cases}
k & \text{if } T_k \leq f(x,y) < T_{k+1}, \quad k = 0, 1, \dots, n \\
n & \text{if } f(x,y) \geq T_n
\end{cases}
$$

**Application**: In MRI brain segmentation, three thresholds separate background, cerebrospinal fluid (CSF), gray matter, and white matter. In CT abdominal imaging, multi-level thresholding isolates air, fat, soft tissue, and bone.

**Kapur's Maximum Entropy Method**: Unlike Otsu which uses variance, Kapur (1985) maximizes the sum of entropies of the two classes:

$$
H(T) = -\sum_{i=0}^{T} \frac{p_i}{\omega_0} \ln\left(\frac{p_i}{\omega_0}\right) - \sum_{i=T+1}^{L-1} \frac{p_i}{\omega_1} \ln\left(\frac{p_i}{\omega_1}\right)
$$

The optimal threshold is:

$$
T^* = \arg\max_{t} H(t)
$$

**Differences from Otsu**:

| Aspect | Otsu | Kapur |
|---|---|---|
| Criterion | Variance-based | Entropy-based |
| Goal | Maximize $\sigma^2_B$ | Maximize entropy $H$ |
| Assumption | Bimodal histogram | Information-theoretic separability |
| Robustness | Good for high-contrast images | Better for noisy low-contrast images |

For multi-level extension to $n$ thresholds, both methods employ exhaustive search or optimization heuristics (genetic algorithms, particle swarm optimization) due to computational complexity.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Clipping** is *value-limiting* (preserves or saturates); **thresholding** is *value-classifying* (binary/label assignment).
- Clipping piecewise function must always include the **clipping condition** — omitting it costs marks.
- Window/Level clipping is the **mathematical heart of CT console displays** — memorize $WL - WW/2$ and $WL + WW/2$.
- Otsu's method: maximize $\sigma^2_B(t) = \omega_0 \omega_1 (\mu_0 - \mu_1)^2$.
- Adaptive thresholding: $T(x,y) = m_{local}(x,y) + C$ where $C$ is a negative bias typically.
- Multi-level thresholding with $n$ thresholds produces $n+1$ segmented regions.
- Hysteresis thresholding uses **two** thresholds $T_{low} < T_{high}$ (canonical in Canny edge detection).
- Otsu assumes **bimodal histogram**; fails on unimodal or multimodal histograms.
- Kapur's method is the entropy-based alternative to Otsu's variance-based approach.
- Percentage clipping is **outlier-robust** — use 1–2% bounds for medical images with metal artifacts.
- Always specify units (gray levels, HU) when stating threshold values in answers.
- The transformation $g$ from clipping is **monotonic non-decreasing**; thresholding is **non-monotonic** and discontinuous.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Digital Negative
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBMT604 - PRINCIPLES OF MEDICAL IMAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Digital Negative

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Digital Negative** (also called **Image Negation** or **Photographic Negative**) is a fundamental **point-processing spatial-domain image enhancement technique** in which the intensity value of every pixel in a grayscale (or single-channel) digital image is inverted with respect to a defined maximum intensity limit. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, it falls under the category of **gray-level transformation functions**, specifically belonging to the family of *linear* (or affine) point operations.

Mathematically, for an input image $f(x,y)$ with pixel intensity range $0 \leq f(x,y) \leq L-1$ (where $L$ is the total number of gray levels), the negative image $g(x,y)$ is generated pixel-by-pixel using the **s-complement transformation**:

$$g(x,y) = s_{\text{max}} - f(x,y)$$

For standard 8-bit medical images, $s_{\text{max}} = 2^n - 1$, where $n$ is the number of bits used to represent a pixel. The most common case in medical imaging is $n = 8$, giving $s_{\text{max}} = \mathbf{255}$, and the standard negative transformation becomes:

$$g(x,y) = 255 - f(x,y)$$

For **12-bit DICOM images** (typical of CT/MRI modalities), $s_{\text{max}} = \mathbf{4095}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Digital Negative is the prototypical example used to introduce students to the concept of gray-level transformation functions $s = T(r)$, where the transformation $T$ operates independently on each pixel intensity $r$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are holding a **standard 35mm photographic film negative** (the old analog "dark slide" used in film cameras before digital sensors). The areas that appeared **bright white** in the real world (like clouds or a snow-capped mountain) appear **dark/black** on the negative. Conversely, the areas that appeared **dark** (like the night sky or a black coat) appear **bright/white** on the negative.

A **Digital Negative** does exactly this — but to a digital medical image stored inside a computer. It is the digital equivalent of that physical "inversion trick" performed by your grandfather's darkroom chemistry.

### Real-World Analogy — The "Whiteboard Eraser" Model
- **Original image** = a blackboard with white chalk drawings on it. The drawings (bright pixels) are clearly visible against the dark background.
- **Digital Negative** = taking a fresh whiteboard and drawing everything in **black marker on a white surface**. The exact same information is preserved, but the contrast is reversed.
- The "shape" of every anatomical structure stays identical; only the **darkness/brightness** roles swap.

### Why Doctors Care About the Negative
Radiologists do not use the negative just for aesthetics. In mammography, for instance, the negative often reveals **microcalcifications and subtle masses** far better than the conventional view, because the human eye is more sensitive to bright spots on a darker background under certain viewing conditions. The transformation directly affects how the **Human Visual System (HVS)** perceives the diagnostic content.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Point for Examiners:** The Digital Negative is **reversible** and **information-preserving**. Applying it twice returns the original image exactly: $f(x,y) = 255 - (255 - f(x,y))$. This reversibility is a frequent KTU viva question.

## 1.3 Standard Constants and Reference Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| Number of gray levels (8-bit) | $L$ | $\mathbf{256}$ | Ultrasound, X-ray film scans |
| Maximum intensity (8-bit) | $s_{\text{max}}$ | $\mathbf{255}$ | Standard DICOM display |
| Number of gray levels (12-bit) | $L$ | $\mathbf{4096}$ | CT slice reconstructions |
| Maximum intensity (12-bit) | $s_{\text{max}}$ | $\mathbf{4095}$ | MRI T1/T2 weighted scans |
| Number of gray levels (16-bit) | $L$ | $\mathbf{65536}$ | Mammography, Radiotherapy |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Logical Breakdown of the Transformation Process

The Digital Negative operation can be decomposed into the following structured engineering steps:

- **Step 1 — Pixel Localization:** The processor visits each pixel coordinate $(x,y)$ of the input image $f$ sequentially (or in parallel for hardware implementations).
- **Step 2 — Intensity Readout:** The current pixel intensity $f(x,y)$ is fetched from memory. For an 8-bit grayscale image, this is an integer in the closed interval $[0, 255]$.
- **Step 3 — Linear Inversion:** The transformation function $T(r) = s_{\text{max}} - r$ is applied. The mathematical complement is computed.
- **Step 4 — Saturation Check:** Although not strictly necessary, a clamping operation ensures the output $g(x,y)$ stays within the valid intensity range $[0, s_{\text{max}}]$. For the negative, this is mathematically guaranteed.
- **Step 5 — Pixel Writeback:** The new intensity $g(x,y)$ is written to the corresponding pixel location in the output image buffer.
- **Step 6 — Iteration:** Steps 1–5 are repeated until every pixel in the $M \times N$ image has been transformed.

### The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why per-pixel (point operation)?** Because the transformation $T$ depends *only* on the current pixel's intensity — it has no dependence on neighboring pixels. This makes the negative trivially parallelizable on a GPU.
- **Why the constant $s_{\text{max}}$?** It is the dynamic range ceiling of the digitization system (the ADC bit-depth). Without subtracting from $s_{\text{max}}$, a simple inversion $g = -f$ would yield only negative numbers, which cannot be stored in unsigned pixel formats.
- **Why is it called "negative"?** Because the resulting image resembles a conventional silver-halide photographic negative when displayed on a calibrated monitor.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Units / Range | Engineering Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | General Negative Transform | $g(x,y) = s_{\text{max}} - f(x,y)$ | Intensity levels (0 to $s_{\text{max}}$) | Core negative transformation |
| 2 | 8-bit Negative | $g = 255 - f$ | Gray levels [0, 255] | Standard radiograph inversion |
| 3 | 12-bit Negative | $g = 4095 - f$ | Gray levels [0, 4095] | CT/MRI slice inversion |
| 4 | Reversibility Identity | $f(x,y) = s_{\text{max}} - g(x,y)$ | Same as input | Verifies lossless property |
| 5 | Normalized Form | $s = 1 - r$, where $r,s \in [0,1]$ | Normalized intensity | Continuous-form analysis |
| 6 | Slope of Transformation | $\dfrac{ds}{dr} = -1$ | Dimensionless | Confirms linear, monotonically decreasing |
| 7 | Mean Intensity After Transform | $\bar{g} = s_{\text{max}} - \bar{f}$ | Intensity levels | Mean shifts to mirror position |
| 8 | Standard Deviation Invariance | $\sigma_g = \sigma_f$ | Intensity levels | Histogram shape is mirrored, not altered in spread |
| 9 | Information Entropy | $H(g) = H(f)$ | Bits/pixel | Entropy preserved — no information loss |
| 10 | Extension to Color (RGB) | $g_c(x,y) = s_{\text{max}} - f_c(x,y)$ for $c \in \{R,G,B\}$ | Per-channel intensity | Photographic negative of color images |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In biomedical engineering practice, the digital negative is far more than a textbook exercise:

- **Mammography Enhancement:** Microcalcifications appear as tiny **bright spots** on a dark fibroglandular background in the conventional view. On the negative, they become dark spots on a bright background — sometimes preferred by certain reading protocols.
- **CT Bone Window Analysis:** Inverted CT slices can convert dense cortical bone (which is white in conventional view) into a dark silhouette, allowing adjacent soft-tissue detail to be inspected without bone overlap.
- **X-ray Film Digitization:** When old analog films are scanned, the digital negative of the scan restores the appearance of the original radiograph that clinicians are trained to interpret.
- **Teaching & Visualization:** Anatomy professors often flip between positive and negative views to emphasize different structural boundaries in lecture slides.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Trick:** Many students confuse "negative" with "thresholding." Remember — **negative is a continuous, smooth, point-wise linear transformation**, while thresholding produces a *binary* (two-level) output. They are fundamentally different operations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Negative Transformation Function

The negative transformation is derived from the requirement of a **monotonically decreasing, linear function** that maps the input intensity interval $[0, s_{\text{max}}]$ onto the output interval $[s_{\text{max}}, 0]$.

Let $r$ be the input pixel intensity and $s$ be the output pixel intensity.

We require two boundary conditions:

$$\text{Condition 1: } T(0) = s_{\text{max}}$$

$$\text{Condition 2: } T(s_{\text{max}}) = 0$$

Assuming a linear form $T(r) = a \cdot r + b$, we substitute the boundary conditions to determine $a$ and $b$.

**Solving for $b$ using Condition 1:**

$$T(0) = a \cdot 0 + b = s_{\text{max}}$$

$$b = s_{\text{max}}$$

**Solving for $a$ using Condition 2:**

$$T(s_{\text{max}}) = a \cdot s_{\text{max}} + b = 0$$

$$a \cdot s_{\text{max}} + s_{\text{max}} = 0$$

$$a \cdot s_{\text{max}} = -s_{\text{max}}$$

$$a = -1$$

**Substituting back into the linear form:**

$$T(r) = -1 \cdot r + s_{\text{max}}$$

$$\boxed{g(x,y) = T(f(x,y)) = s_{\text{max}} - f(x,y)}$$

This completes the derivation. The negative slope $a = -1$ confirms that the function is **linearly decreasing** across the entire dynamic range.

## 3.2 Verification of Lossless / Reversible Property

A fundamental property of the negative transform is **information preservation**. We prove this by applying the transformation twice.

**Apply the negative transform once:**

$$g(x,y) = s_{\text{max}} - f(x,y)$$

**Apply the negative transform again to the result:**

$$g'(x,y) = s_{\text{max}} - g(x,y)$$

**Substitute the expression for $g(x,y)$:**

$$g'(x,y) = s_{\text{max}} - (s_{\text{max}} - f(x,y))$$

**Distribute the negative sign:**

$$g'(x,y) = s_{\text{max}} - s_{\text{max}} + f(x,y)$$

**Simplify:**

$$g'(x,y) = f(x,y)$$

Thus, applying the digital negative transformation twice yields the **exact original image**, confirming the operation is **fully reversible** and **lossless**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Demonstration

Consider a **4-bit grayscale image** (16 gray levels, $s_{\text{max}} = 15$) with the following $4 \times 4$ pixel matrix:

$$f(x,y) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 4 & 8 & 12 \\ 1 & 5 & 9 & 13 \\ 2 & 6 & 10 & 14 \\ 3 & 7 & 11 & 15 \end{bmatrix}$$

Applying $g(x,y) = 15 - f(x,y)$ element-by-element:

**First row:** $15-0=15$, $\;15-4=11$, $\;15-8=7$, $\;15-12=3$

**Second row:** $15-1=14$, $\;15-5=10$, $\;15-9=6$, $\;15-13=2$

**Third row:** $15-2=13$, $\;15-6=9$, $\;15-10=5$, $\;15-14=1$

**Fourth row:** $15-3=12$, $\;15-7=8$, $\;15-11=4$, $\;15-15=0$

Therefore, the resulting negative image is:

$$g(x,y) = \begin{bmatrix} 15 & 11 & 7 & 3 \\ 14 & 10 & 6 & 2 \\ 13 & 9 & 5 & 1 \\ 12 & 8 & 4 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Statistical Verification

**Mean of original:**

$$\bar{f} = \frac{1}{16} \sum_{i=0}^{15} i = \frac{0+1+2+\dots+15}{16} = \frac{120}{16} = 7.5$$

**Mean of negative:**

$$\bar{g} = \frac{1}{16} \sum_{i=0}^{15} (15-i) = \frac{15+14+13+\dots+0}{16} = \frac{120}{16} = 7.5$$

This confirms the identity $\bar{g} = s_{\text{max}} - \bar{f} \Rightarrow 7.5 = 15 - 7.5$ ✓

**Standard Deviation Invariance Check:**

For both matrices (which are arithmetic sequences), the spread from the mean is identical. Thus $\sigma_g = \sigma_f$, confirming that the transformation only **mirrors** the histogram about the central axis at $s_{\text{max}}/2$ without changing its spread.

## 3.4 Extension to Normalized Continuous Domain

In many KTU exam problems, intensities are expressed in the normalized range $[0, 1]$. Under this convention:

$$r = \frac{f(x,y)}{s_{\text{max}}}, \quad s = \frac{g(x,y)}{s_{\text{max}}}$$

The negative transformation becomes:

$$s = 1 - r$$

This form is especially convenient for plotting transformation curves and for blending with other intensity transformation functions (log, power-law) within the same normalized framework.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Flow Architecture of the Digital Negative Operation

The following Mermaid block-diagram maps the complete data-flow pipeline of how an input medical image is transformed into its digital negative by an image processing system. It captures the per-pixel processing logic, the memory read/write cycle, and the boundary-saturation safeguard.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Medical Image Input f of size MxN] --> B[Initialize Output Buffer g]
    B --> C{For Each Pixel x,y}
    C --> D[Read Pixel Intensity f from x,y]
    D --> E[Determine Bit Depth n]
    E --> F[Compute smax equals 2 to the power n minus 1]
    F --> G[Apply Transform g equals smax minus f]
    G --> H{Clamp Check 0 to smax}
    H --> I[Write g to Output Buffer]
    I --> J{All Pixels Processed}
    J -->|No| C
    J -->|Yes| K[Display or Save Negative Image]
    K --> L[Radiologist or CAD System Review]

    style A fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000000
    style K fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#000000
    style L fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000000
    style G fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000000
```

## 4.2 Gray-Level Transformation Curve

The transformation characteristic is plotted below. Note that it is a **straight line with slope $-1$** that intersects the vertical axis at $s_{\text{max}}$ and the horizontal axis at $s_{\text{max}}$ — i.e., it is the linear segment connecting $(0, s_{\text{max}})$ and $(s_{\text{max}}, 0)$.

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1["Input r equals 0"] --- Q1["Output s equals smax"]
    P2["Input r equals 1/4 smax"] --- Q2["Output s equals 3/4 smax"]
    P3["Input r equals 1/2 smax"] --- Q3["Output s equals 1/2 smax"]
    P4["Input r equals 3/4 smax"] --- Q4["Output s equals 1/4 smax"]
    P5["Input r equals smax"] --- Q5["Output s equals 0"]

    style P1 fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,color:#000000
    style Q1 fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000000
    style P3 fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,color:#000000
    style Q3 fill:#F5F5F5,stroke:#424242,color:#000000
    style P5 fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,color:#000000
    style Q5 fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000000
```

## 4.3 Histogram Mirror Effect — Conceptual Diagram

The digital negative produces a **mirror-image reflection** of the input histogram about the central vertical axis at intensity $s_{\text{max}}/2$. The shape, area, and standard deviation are preserved; only the orientation flips.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ORIGINAL["HISTOGRAM OF f"]
        H1["Dark Region Peak"]
        H2["Mid Gray Tonal Spread"]
        H3["Bright Region Tail"]
    end
    subgraph NEGATIVE["HISTOGRAM OF g"]
        N1["Bright Region Peak"]
        N2["Mid Gray Tonal Spread"]
        N3["Dark Region Tail"]
    end
    H1 --> MIRROR{Mirror Axis at smax by 2}
    H2 --> MIRROR
    H3 --> MIRROR
    MIRROR --> N1
    MIRROR --> N2
    MIRROR --> N3

    style H1 fill:#212121,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF
    style H3 fill:#FAFAFA,stroke:#9E9E9E,color:#000000
    style N1 fill:#FAFAFA,stroke:#9E9E9E,color:#000000
    style N3 fill:#212121,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF
    style MIRROR fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q: Define "Digital Negative" in the context of medical image processing. Write its mathematical expression for an 8-bit grayscale image.**

**Model Answer:**

A digital negative is a point-processing intensity transformation in which each pixel value is replaced by its complement with respect to the maximum allowed intensity. For an 8-bit grayscale image, the transformation is:

$$g(x,y) = 255 - f(x,y)$$

It enhances bright anatomical structures by converting them into dark regions and vice versa, often useful in mammography and CT bone-window analysis. **(3 Marks)**

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q: State any three properties of the digital negative transformation.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Reversibility:** Applying the transformation twice yields the original image: $f = 255 - (255 - f)$. **(1 Mark)**
2. **Slope:** The transformation is linear with slope $-1$, meaning it is monotonically decreasing. **(1 Mark)**
3. **Histogram Mirror Effect:** The output histogram is a mirror reflection of the input histogram about the axis $s_{\text{max}}/2$, preserving standard deviation and entropy. **(1 Mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** For each module question, the student is given an **OR choice** between two questions. We provide both alternatives below.

### Question A (14 Marks) — CHOICE 1
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question Bank, Module 2]**

**Q: (a)** Explain the concept of digital negative in medical image processing with a suitable mathematical formulation. Derive the expression and discuss its slope characteristic. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 3-bit grayscale chest X-ray image contains a $2 \times 4$ region of interest with the following pixel values:

$$f(x,y) = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 5 & 6 & 1 \\ 0 & 3 & 7 & 4 \end{bmatrix}$$

Compute the digital negative $g(x,y)$ and verify that applying the negative operation again restores the original matrix. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

A digital negative is the **pixel-wise intensity complement** of an image, given by:

$$g(x,y) = (2^n - 1) - f(x,y)$$

For 8-bit images, $2^n - 1 = 255$, giving $g(x,y) = 255 - f(x,y)$. **(2 Marks)**

**Derivation:** Let $T(r) = ar + b$ be linear with boundary conditions $T(0) = s_{\text{max}}$ and $T(s_{\text{max}}) = 0$. Solving yields $a = -1$, $b = s_{\text{max}}$, giving $T(r) = s_{\text{max}} - r$. **(3 Marks)**

**Slope Characteristic:** The slope $\dfrac{ds}{dr} = -1$ indicates a constant negative rate of change across the entire dynamic range. This makes the transform **linear, monotonically decreasing**, and **fully reversible** (inverse is itself). The negative slope causes dark pixels to map bright, and bright pixels to map dark — producing a photographic-negative look-up table. **(2 Marks)**

### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

For a **3-bit image**, $s_{\text{max}} = 2^3 - 1 = 7$.

The transformation is $g(x,y) = 7 - f(x,y)$. Apply to each element:

**First row:**
- $7 - 2 = 5$
- $7 - 5 = 2$
- $7 - 6 = 1$
- $7 - 1 = 6$

**Second row:**
- $7 - 0 = 7$
- $7 - 3 = 4$
- $7 - 7 = 0$
- $7 - 4 = 3$

Therefore, the digital negative is:

$$g(x,y) = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 2 & 1 & 6 \\ 7 & 4 & 0 & 3 \end{bmatrix} \quad \textbf{(3 Marks)}$$

**Verification of Reversibility:** Apply the negative again: $g'(x,y) = 7 - g(x,y)$.

- First row: $7-5=2$, $\;7-2=5$, $\;7-1=6$, $\;7-6=1$
- Second row: $7-7=0$, $\;7-4=3$, $\;7-0=7$, $\;7-3=4$

$$g'(x,y) = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 5 & 6 & 1 \\ 0 & 3 & 7 & 4 \end{bmatrix} = f(x,y) \checkmark \quad \textbf{(2 Marks)}$$

The original matrix is exactly recovered, confirming the **lossless reversibility** of the digital negative. **(2 Marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — CHOICE 2 (OR)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question Bank, Module 2]**

**Q: (a)** With a neat labeled sketch of the transformation function, discuss the digital negative as a gray-level transformation. Compare it with image thresholding. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An 8-bit MRI image has a histogram with most pixel intensities concentrated in the dark range $[20, 60]$. Explain qualitatively what effect the digital negative transformation will have on the visual appearance of this image, and justify whether it is diagnostically useful. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

The digital negative is plotted as a **straight line with slope $-1$** passing through points $(0, 255)$ and $(255, 0)$ on the $s$ vs $r$ plane. **(1 Mark)**

**Diagram description (to be drawn in answer script):**

```
s (output)
255 |*
    |  *
    |    *
    |      *
    |        *
    |          *
    |            *
    |              *
    |                *
    |                  *
  0 |____________________*
    0                  255    r (input)
```

**(2 Marks)** for the labeled sketch.

**Comparison with Thresholding:** **(4 Marks)**

| Aspect | Digital Negative | Thresholding |
|---|---|---|
| Operation type | Continuous linear mapping | Discrete binary mapping |
| Output levels | All 256 levels preserved | Only 2 levels (0 and 255) |
| Formula | $g = 255 - f$ | $g = 255$ if $f > T$, else $g = 0$ |
| Reversibility | Fully reversible | Not reversible |
| Information | Preserved | Lost (binarization) |
| Computational cost | One subtraction per pixel | One comparison per pixel |

### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

The original MRI has most pixel intensities in the **dark range $[20, 60]$** of the 8-bit scale, meaning the anatomical structures of interest are dim against a black background. **(1 Mark)**

After applying $g(x,y) = 255 - f(x,y)$, the dark pixel range $[20, 60]$ becomes the **bright range $[195, 235]$**. The structures now appear as **bright regions on a near-black background** (since very low intensities near 0 map to near 255). **(2 Marks)**

**Visual Effect:** The image will look like a **classic photographic negative**, with anatomical tissue showing as bright tones and air/background appearing dark. Contrast between different soft-tissue regions may improve because the eye is sensitive to brightness variations in the high-intensity regime. **(2 Marks)**
