# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]

**"List any three quantitative metrics commonly used to evaluate medical image segmentation algorithms. State the ideal value and direction (higher/lower is better) for each."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

The three most commonly used quantitative metrics for evaluating medical image segmentation are:

1. **Dice Coefficient (Dice Similarity Coefficient / F1-Score for segmentation):**
   - **Range:** $[0, 1]$
   - **Direction:** **Higher is better**.
   - **Ideal value:** $1.0$ (perfect overlap with ground truth).
   - **Formula:** $\text{Dice} = \frac{2 \cdot \vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert}$, where $A$ is the predicted mask and $B$ is the ground truth.

2. **Intersection over Union (IoU / Jaccard Index):**
   - **Range:** $[0, 1]$
   - **Direction:** **Higher is better**.
   - **Ideal value:** $1.0$.
   - **Formula:** $\text{IoU} = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$.

3. **Hausdorff Distance 95th Percentile (HD95):**
   - **Range:** $[0, \infty)$, measured in **mm** or **pixels**.
   - **Direction:** **Lower is better**.
   - **Ideal value:** $0$ (boundary of prediction exactly matches ground truth).
   - **Formula:** $\text{HD}_{95} = \max\!\left(\max_{a \in A} d_{95\%}(a, B),\, \max_{b \in B} d_{95\%}(b, A)\right)$.

> *(End — 3 marks awarded: 1 mark for each metric with formula and direction.)*

---

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]

**"What is PSNR? Why is it considered insufficient as a standalone metric for medical image quality assessment?"** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
**Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio (PSNR)** is a logarithmic, decibel-scale measure of the ratio between the maximum possible signal power of an image and the power of the corrupting noise, defined as:

$$ \text{PSNR} = 20 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\text{MAX}_I}{\sqrt{\text{MSE}}}\right)\ \text{dB} $$

where $\text{MAX}_I$ is the peak pixel value (e.g., 255 for 8-bit images) and **MSE** is the Mean Squared Error between the reconstructed and ground-truth images.

**Why PSNR is insufficient alone (any 2 of the following 3 reasons):**

1. **Pixel-wise blindness to structure:** PSNR is computed from **per-pixel squared differences**, so it treats each pixel independently. Two images can have the same PSNR but vastly different perceptual or structural quality (e.g., one with a blurred tumor boundary and one with sharp diagnostic detail).

2. **Insensitive to perceptual / human-visual quality:** Medical diagnosis depends on *structural patterns* (lesion shape, texture, edge gradients), not on per-pixel intensity matching. PSNR does not model the **Human Visual System (HVS)** contrast sensitivity.

3. **Contradictions with clinical perception:** A denoised MRI may have a *high* PSNR but a *blurred* tumor boundary, leading to a missed diagnosis. A complementary metric such as **SSIM** (which captures luminance, contrast, and structure) or **LPIPS** (learned perceptual) must always be reported alongside PSNR in MIP projects.

> *(End — 3 marks awarded: 1 for definition, 2 for insufficiency reasons.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### Question B-A. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme, Module 4]

**Part (a)** — *Design the structure of a 12-minute project defense presentation for an MIP project titled "Lung Nodule Detection in CT Images Using an Enhanced Faster R-CNN." Specify the slide sequence, content of each slide, and the time budget per slide. Justify why each slide is necessary.* (7 Marks)

**Part (b)** — *For the same project, the team reports a sensitivity of 0.94 and a specificity of 0.81 on a test set of 1000 CT slices containing 120 confirmed nodules. Compute the values of TP, TN, FP, FN, Accuracy, and F1-score. Comment on whether the technique is clinically acceptable.* (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

The presentation should be structured as a **clinical-rounds narrative** over 9 content slides + 1 title + 1 thank-you slide, with the following time budget:

| Slide # | Title | Time (sec) | Content | Justification |
|---------|-------|-----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | **Title Slide** | 30 | Project title, team, guide, KTU logo, date | Standard academic protocol. |
| 2 | **Outline** | 30 | 6 bullets summarizing the talk | Lets the panel track the talk. |
| 3 | **Motivation** | 90 | Lung cancer mortality statistic + sample CT with a nodule circled + research objective | Hooks the panel emotionally and clinically. |
| 4 | **Literature Survey** | 90 | 3-row table: Method + Limitation; ends with research gap | Establishes novelty and academic maturity. |
| 5 | **Proposed Methodology** | 180 | Block diagram (Input → Preprocessing → Faster R-CNN with FPN → Postprocessing → Output) + the FPN key equation | Core technical content; must dominate the time budget. |
| 6 | **Key Equation** | 60 | The Faster R-CNN RPN loss equation: $L = L_{cls} + L_{box}$ | Reinforces algorithm understanding. |
| 7 | **Dataset & Implementation** | 60 | LIDC-IDRI dataset, 1000 slices, 80/10/10 split, PyTorch, RTX 3060 | Validates reproducibility. |
| 8 | **Qualitative Results** | 90 | 5-panel: Input \| Ground Truth \| Baseline \| Proposed \| Overlay | Visual evidence is the most memorable proof. |
| 9 | **Quantitative + Ablation** | 90 | Results table (bold best), ablation table (backbone variants) | Numerical evidence + causal justification. |
| 10 | **Applications & Limitations** | 60 | 3 clinical use cases, 2 honest limitations, 2 future directions | Demonstrates translational maturity. |
| 11 | **Thank You / Q&A** | 120 | Contact, GitHub QR code | Standard academic closure. |

**Total: 12 minutes.**

**Justification (sample 2 sentences per slide to defend if questioned):**
- *Slide 3 (Motivation):* Without a clinical hook, the panel cannot assess the *societal relevance* of the work. A patient-impact statistic is non-negotiable.
- *Slide 5 (Methodology):* The block diagram is the single most important visual. If a panelist can sketch your pipeline back to you, you have succeeded.
- *Slide 9 (Quantitative + Ablation):* A results table without an ablation is suspicious — the panel will assume the gain is accidental.

> *(End — 7 marks: 4 for slide structure & content, 2 for time budget, 1 for justification.)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Test set $N = 1000$ CT slices
- Total positive cases (nodules present) = $\text{P} = 120$
- Total negative cases = $\text{N} = 1000 - 120 = 880$
- Sensitivity (Recall) = $\text{Se} = 0.94$
- Specificity = $\text{Sp} = 0.81$

**Step 1 — Compute TP, FN, TN, FP.**

By definition of sensitivity:

$$ \text{Se} = \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FN}} = \frac{\text{TP}}{120} = 0.94 $$

$$ \text{TP} = 0.94 \times 120 = 112.8 \approx 113 $$

$$ \text{FN} = 120 - 113 = 7 $$

By definition of specificity:

$$ \text{Sp} = \frac{\text{TN}}{\text{TN} + \text{FP}} = \frac{\text{TN}}{880} = 0.81 $$

$$ \text{TN} = 0.81 \times 880 = 712.8 \approx 713 $$

$$ \text{FP} = 880 - 713 = 167 $$

**Step 2 — Compute Accuracy.**

$$ \text{Accuracy} = \frac{\text{TP} + \text{TN}}{\text{TP} + \text{TN} + \text{FP} + \text{FN}} = \frac{113 + 713}{113 + 713 + 167 + 7} = \frac{826}{1000} = 0.826 = 82.6\% $$

**Step 3 — Compute Precision.**

$$ \text{Precision} = \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FP}} = \frac{113}{113 + 167} = \frac{113}{280} = 0.4036 = 40.4\% $$

**Step 4 — Compute F1-Score.**

$$ F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot \text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}} = \frac{2 \times 0.4036 \times 0.94}{0.4036 + 0.94} = \frac{0.7588}{1.3436} = 0.5647 \approx 0.565 $$

**Step 5 — Clinical Acceptability Comment.**

While the **sensitivity of 0.94 is clinically excellent** (the model misses only 7 out of 120 nodules — acceptable for a screening tool), the **specificity of 0.81 and the F1-score of 0.565 are weak**, because there are **167 false positives** in only 880 negative slices. This means for every correctly identified nodule, the model raises ~1.5 false alarms, leading to **radiologist fatigue and unnecessary follow-up scans**.

**Verdict:** The technique is **clinically acceptable as a first-pass screening tool** (high sensitivity is essential for cancer detection — *missing a cancer is worse than a false alarm*), but it is **not acceptable as a stand-alone diagnostic tool** without an additional false-positive reduction stage (e.g., a 3D-CNN false-positive classifier). The team should propose this as future work.

> *(End — 7 marks: 1 for set-up, 2 for TP/TN/FP/FN, 1 for accuracy, 1 for F1, 2 for clinical comment.)*

---

### Question B-B. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme, Module 4 — Alternative Choice]

**Part (a)** — *Explain the "Clinical Rounds" six-section narrative architecture for presenting a Medical Image Processing (MIP) project. For each section, state its purpose, time budget, and what a KTU evaluation panel expects to hear.* (7 Marks)

**Part (b)** — *A denoising algorithm is evaluated on a brain MRI test set. The ground truth (noise-free) image $I$ and the denoised output $\hat{I}$ both have pixel values in the range $[0, 255]$. The MSE between them is computed as 72.5. Calculate the PSNR in dB. If a competing method achieves PSNR = 31.8 dB on the same dataset, which method is better and by approximately what margin (in terms of noise power reduction)?* (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

The "Clinical Rounds" six-section narrative architecture mirrors the **morning case-presentation style of a hospital** and is the recommended structure for a KTU MIP project defense. The six sections are:

**Section 1 — Clinical/Motivational Hook (90 sec)**
- **Purpose:** Establish the *real-world medical problem* with a patient-impact statistic and a representative pathology image.
- **Panel Expectation:** Conviction that the problem is *frequent, costly, and clinically important*.

**Section 2 — Literature Survey & Positioning (90 sec)**
- **Purpose:** A 3-row comparative table of existing methods and their limitations, ending with the **research gap** your work fills.
- **Panel Expectation:** Evidence of academic maturity; proof of novelty.

**Section 3 — Proposed Methodology (180–240 sec)**
- **Purpose:** A block diagram and the key equation(s) of your technique; dataset, framework, and hardware details.
- **Panel Expectation:** Confirmation that the team *understands* what they implemented, not just copy-pasted from a GitHub repository.

**Section 4 — Results & Comparative Analysis (180 sec)**
- **Purpose:** Qualitative (5-panel visual comparison) and quantitative (results table with PSNR/SSIM/Dice/Accuracy) results, with the best result in **bold**.
- **Panel Expectation:** Hard numerical proof that the technique *beats baselines*.

**Section 5 — Effectiveness Highlight & Ablation (90 sec)**
- **Purpose:** An ablation table showing which component contributed what gain (e.g., with vs. without skip connections), plus optional Grad-CAM/attention maps for interpretability.
- **Panel Expectation:** Proof that the gain is *causal*, not accidental hyperparameter tuning.

**Section 6 — Clinical Applications, Limitations & Future Scope (60 sec)**
- **Purpose:** 3 clinical deployment scenarios, 2 honest limitations, 2 future directions.
- **Panel Expectation:** Translational maturity and engineering responsibility.

> *(End — 7 marks: 6 × 1 mark for each section's purpose + expectation, 1 mark for time-budget integration.)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Bit-depth $b = 8$, so $\text{MAX}_I = 2^8 - 1 = 255$.
- $\text{MSE} = 72.5$ (between ground truth and denoised output).

**Step 1 — Apply the PSNR formula.**

$$ \text{PSNR} = 20 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\text{MAX}_I}{\sqrt{\text{MSE}}}\right) $$

**Step 2 — Compute $\sqrt{\text{MSE}}$.**

$$ \sqrt{\text{MSE}} = \sqrt{72.5} \approx 8.515 $$

**Step 3 — Compute the ratio.**

$$ \frac{\text{MAX}_I}{\sqrt{\text{MSE}}} = \frac{255}{8.515} \approx 29.95 $$

**Step 4 — Compute the logarithm and multiply.**

$$ \log_{10}(29.95) \approx 1.4764 $$

$$ \text{PSNR} = 20 \times 1.4764 = 29.53\ \text{dB} $$

**Step 5 — Compare with the competing method.**

The competing method achieves $\text{PSNR}_{comp} = 31.8\ \text{dB}$. Since **higher PSNR is better**, the competing method is superior.

**Step 6 — Compute the noise-power margin.**

Recall that PSNR is in dB: $\text{PSNR} = 10 \log_{10}(\text{MAX}_I^{\,2} / \text{MSE})$. The difference in dB:

$$ \Delta\text{PSNR} = 31.8 - 29.53 = 2.27\ \text{dB} $$

Convert this dB difference to a *linear* noise-power ratio. Using $10^{\Delta\text{PSNR}/10}$:

$$ \frac{\text{MSE}_{ours}}{\text{MSE}_{comp}} = 10^{\Delta\text{PSNR}/10} = 10^{0.227} \approx 1.686 $$

This means **our method has ~1.69 times more noise power** than the competing method (equivalently, the competing method has ~40% less noise power than ours).

> *(End — 7 marks: 2 for PSNR formula setup, 2 for numerical computation, 1 for comparison verdict, 2 for noise-power margin computation.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Putting derivations in the main slides** — derivations belong in the **report and Q&A**, not the presentation slides. Slides with full derivations = 0 marks for visual communication.
> 2. **Reporting only one metric** — always report *at least two* metrics (e.g., PSNR + SSIM, or Dice + HD95). Reporting only PSNR in a segmentation project is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
> 3. **No ablation study** — the panel will assume the improvement is *hyperparameter luck*. Always include a component-wise ablation.
> 4. **Burying the result** — the best metric value should be in **bold** in a font ≥ 14 pt. If the panel has to hunt for your number, you lose impact.
> 5. **Ignoring clinical context** — a project that reports Dice 0.91 with no mention of clinical workflow, radiologist workload, or deployment cost will be flagged as *"technically correct, clinically immature."*
> 6. **Failing to defend the test set** — the panel will ask *"Is your test set seen or unseen?"* If you trained and tested on the same patients (data leakage), your entire result is invalid. State the train/val/test split *explicitly*.
> 7. **Running over time** — going 18 minutes when allotted 12 is the #1 presentation killer. **Rehearse with a stopwatch.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Project Presentation in Medical Image Processing (Module 4)**

- [ ] **Definition:** A *Project Defense Presentation (PDP)* is a 10–15 min oral-visual technical defense of an MIP project, evaluated on **Technical Validity (40%) + Visual Communication (35%) + Translational Relevance (25%)**.

- [ ] **Six-Section Narrative Architecture:** *Motivation → Literature → Methodology → Results → Ablation → Applications*.

- [ ] **Time Budget Rule:** **8–10 content slides for 12 min**, with **70–80 sec per slide**.

- [ ] **Core Metrics to Defend (with formulas in latex):**
  - PSNR (dB, ↑), SSIM ([0,1], ↑), MSE (↓)
  - Dice ([0,1], ↑), IoU ([0,1], ↑), HD95 (mm, ↓)
  - Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity, F1, AUC-ROC

- [ ] **The "So What?" Framework:** Always map your technique to a stage in the clinical imaging pipeline: *Acquisition → Reconstruction → Pre-processing → Algorithm → Post-processing → Segmentation → Diagnosis → Treatment*.

- [ ] **The Clinical Rounds Analogy:** History → Investigations → Procedure → Findings → Vitals → Discharge Plan.

- [ ] **Must-Have Slides for KTU Grade:**
  1. Title, 2. Outline, 3. Motivation + Objective, 4. Literature + Gap, 5. Methodology Block Diagram, 6. Key Equation, 7. Dataset + Implementation, 8. Qualitative Results, 9. Quantitative + Ablation, 10. Applications + Limitations, 11. Thank You.

- [ ] **Three Pillars of a Great Presentation:** *Technical Validity, Visual Communication, Translational Relevance*.

- [ ] **Pitfalls to Avoid:** Derivations on slides, single metric, no ablation, hidden best result, no clinical context, data leakage, running over time.

- [ ] **Final Confidence Mantra:** *"A great MIP presentation does not show how smart you are — it shows the audience, clearly, quickly, and honestly, how well your technique solves a real medical imaging problem."*

- [ ] **Examiner's Heuristic:** A panel that hears *Remember/Understand* answers grades the project **routine**; a panel that hears *Analyse/Evaluate/Create* answers grades it **innovative** and gives full marks.

- [ ] **Closing Reminder for the Day of Presentation:** Rehearse with a stopwatch, drink water before speaking, breathe after each slide, smile at the panel, and **never read from the slide** — *the slide supports you, not the other way around.*
